title: Pay slips

meta.description: Pay slip requirements for Australian employers — what must be included and when they must be provided.

headings.text: When must a pay slip be given?
What must a pay slip include?
What must NOT be on a pay slip

content:
All employees must receive a pay slip within one business day of being paid, whether pay day falls on a weekend or public holiday or not.

Pay slips can be provided in electronic or hard copy form. Electronic pay slips must contain the same information as paper pay slips.

What a pay slip MUST include (mandatory under Fair Work Regulations 2009):
1. Employer's name
2. Employer's ABN (if applicable)
3. Employee's name
4. Date of payment
5. Pay period (the period of employment to which the pay slip relates)
6. Gross and net pay amounts
7. Any loadings, monetary allowances, bonuses, incentive payments, penalty rates or other separately identifiable entitlements paid in the pay period
8. Hourly rate of pay (if the employee is paid by the hour)
9. Number of ordinary hours worked (if paid by the hour)
10. Any deductions — must specify the name (or name and number) of the fund or account to which each deduction was paid
11. Superannuation contributions — amount, period, date(s) paid, name of super fund

For salaried employees: the pay slip must include the employee's annual salary rate.

What must NOT appear on a pay slip:
- Any reference to paid family and domestic violence leave taken (to protect employee privacy)

Format requirements:
- Must be in English
- Electronic format is acceptable (email, employee self-service portal etc.)
- Must be legible and in a format the employee can access

Leave balances:
Employers need to keep records of leave balances and leave taken by employees. While leave balances do not have to appear on pay slips under law, many employers include them as best practice (and some awards require it).

Penalties:
Failure to issue a pay slip within one business day of payment is a breach of the Fair Work Act. Inspectors can issue infringement notices on the spot. Penalties can apply per breach. Deliberate failure to provide pay slips may attract civil penalty proceedings.
tables: Family and domestic violence leave taken

key_facts.legislation: Fair Work Act 2009 (Cth); Fair Work Regulations 2009
